title: CRAN Submission Checklist

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Start Phase 1: Pre-Release Preparation Phase 2: Local Validation Phase 3: Cross-Platform Testing Phase 4: Final Review Phase 5: Submission Post-Submission Common Rejection Reasons Helpful Resources Note Function Syntax: Development functions auto-loaded .Rprofile start R package directory. can call directly (e.g., dev_check_complete()) without using source(). ’ve disabled .Rprofile, use source("dev/function_name.R") instead.

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Timing requirements: - [ ] 30-day minimum CRAN releases (unless fixing critical bugs) - [ ] planned features complete tested - [ ] bugs previous version addressed - [ ] Breaking changes documented NEWS.md First-time submissions: - [ ] Expect longer review times (1-2 weeks vs 1-3 days updates) - [ ] extra thorough - first impressions matter - [ ] cran-comments.md ready explain package

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Update version number DESCRIPTION Major.Minor.Patch (e.g., 1.0.0, 1.1.0, 1.0.1) Remove .9000 development suffix Follow semantic versioning Update NEWS.md changes
code: # Script: Manual editing required # Group changes by: New features, Bug fixes, Breaking changes, Deprecations

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Critical formatting (CRAN strict !): Use Title Case period end Remove “R” “R” (redundant CRAN) Max ~65 characters Example: Title: Fast Data Processing Large Datasets Multiple complete sentences elaboration “package…” opening package name first word Capitalize software/package names correctly Use parentheses function names: function() Quote external packages correct casing: 'shiny', 'ggplot2' Example: contributors listed correct roles least one cre (maintainer) valid email Include cph (copyright holder) applicable Roles: aut (author), cre (maintainer), ctb (contributor), cph (copyright holder) Valid CRAN license (MIT, GPL-3, etc.) MIT: ensure LICENSE file exists Use HTTPS exclusively (HTTP) Format URLs angle brackets Description: <https://...> Canonical CRAN package links: https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=pkgname Example: used packages listed Imports Suggests unnecessary dependencies Minimum R version specified using recent features Consider using attachment package: attachment::att_amend_desc()
code: Description: Provides efficient algorithms for processing large datasets.     Implements parallel computing techniques to reduce computation time.     Includes visualization tools for exploring results. URL: https://github.com/username/pkgname, https://username.github.io/pkgname/ BugReports: https://github.com/username/pkgname/issues

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Run spell check Add technical terms inst/WORDLIST (one per line) Package names quotes match exact casing typos titles, descriptions, documentation Function documentation completeness Every exported function @return tag (required!) @param tags descriptions empty documentation fields Internal functions use #' @noRd partially documented Check : checkhelper::find_missing_tags() (package installed) Examples quality examples run successfully Replace \dontrun{} (interactive()) {} interactive-code Use \donttest{} slow examples (>5 seconds) Examples demonstrate typical use cases Avoid examples requiring external resources (APIs, databases) Vignettes/Articles (present): code chunks execute without errors absolute file paths references local files Build successfully:

title: CRAN Submission Checklist

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Problem: Improper formatting Problem: URLs angle brackets Problem: Package names quoted correctly
code: # Bad Title: An R Package for Data Analysis. Description: This package analyzes data.  # Good Title: Fast Data Analysis Tools Description: Provides efficient algorithms for analyzing large datasets.     Implements parallel computing techniques to reduce computation time. # Bad (in Description field) See https://example.com for details.  # Good See <https://example.com> for details. # Bad Uses the Shiny framework...  # Good Uses the 'shiny' framework...

title: CRAN Submission Checklist
text: Problem: Missing @return tags Problem: Overuse \dontrun{}
code: # Bad #' Calculate mean #' @param x numeric vector foo <- function(x) mean(x)  # Good #' Calculate mean #' @param x numeric vector #' @return The arithmetic mean of x foo <- function(x) mean(x) # Bad #' @examples #' \dontrun{ #'   foo(1:10) #' }  # Good #' @examples #' foo(1:10) #' #' # For interactive-only code: #' if (interactive()) { #'   foo(1:10) #' }

title: CRAN Submission Checklist

code: # Bad - will be rejected! write.csv(data, "~/output.csv") write.csv(data, "output.csv")  # Good - write to temporary directory tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv") write.csv(data, tmp) # ... use file ... unlink(tmp)  # Clean up

title: communities
text: communities dataset contains spatially-thinned plant community records Sierra Nevada mountain range SE Spain, compiled official vegetation map 1:10,000 scale. designed support multi-class classification, binary presence-absence modelling, ecological niche analysis using climate topographic predictors. main features : Sierra Nevada scope: 7,300 point records covering Sierra Nevada mountain range (SE Spain, EPSG:25830). Multi-class binary responses: community column 6 levels ("none" + 5 named plant communities) 5 binary presence-absence columns individual community types. Climate topographic predictors: 9 numeric predictors spanning seasonal temperature extremes, rainfall, terrain attributes. Temporal climate scenario rasters: associated rasters baseline (2010), 2050, 2100 climate scenarios available via download functions.

text: model Brier score 0.213, reasonably good, indicating model producing well-calibrated class probabilities. However, score says nothing per-class performance. understand well model predicts class, first need transform --bag class probabilities class predictions. code extracts name column communities_rf$predictions highest probability per observation. Now use observed predicted data build confusion matrix showing model performance per class community. , use diagonal confusion matrix compute model’s overall recall (sensitivity true positive rate), matrix rows compute per-class recall. model achieves -random recall communities, particularly accurate discriminating juniper-broom shrublands alpine pastures.

code: area_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   terra::freq(prediction_2010) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2010),   terra::freq(prediction_2050) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2050),   terra::freq(prediction_2100) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2100) ) |>   dplyr::rename(community = value) |>   #resolution is 100x100 meters = 1 ha per cell   dplyr::mutate(hectares = count) |>   dplyr::select(year, community, hectares) |>    dplyr::filter(community != "none")  ggplot2::ggplot(   data = area_df,   ggplot2::aes(     x = year,     y = hectares,     colour = community,     group  = community   ) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3) +   ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = communities_colors) +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2010, 2050, 2100)) +   ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Year",     y = "Area (hectares)",     colour = "Community"   ) +   ggplot2::theme_bw() #stack together projections for 2010 and 2100 transition_df <- terra::as.data.frame(   x = c(prediction_2010, prediction_2050),    na.rm = TRUE   ) |>   setNames(c("communities_2010", "communities_2050")) |>   #count pixels transitioning from one community to another   dplyr::count(     from = communities_2010,      to = communities_2050,      name = "hectares"     ) |>   dplyr::group_by(from) |>   dplyr::mutate(     transition_percent = round(       x = hectares * 100 / sum(hectares),        digits = 1       )     ) |>   dplyr::select(-hectares) |>    dplyr::ungroup() |>    tidyr::pivot_wider(     names_from = "to",     values_from = "transition_percent",     values_fill = 0   ) |>    dplyr::filter(     from != "none"   ) |>    dplyr::rename(     Community = from   )  knitr::kable(transition_df)

title: neanderthal
text: section fit weighted ensemble quasibinomial GAM models case weights. Let’s unpack ! Case weights: neanderthal dataframe 49 178 absences, imbalance might result overprediction zeros. mitigate issue, function collinear::case_weights() generates inverse-frequency weights classes contribute equally model fit. Quasibinomial GAM: GAM models replace linear terms used GLMs smoothing splines (s()) better represent non-linear relationships presence environment. use quasibinomial family accounts overdispersion introduced pseudo-absence data case weights. basis dimension set k = floor(n_presences/4) capture unimodal monotonic responses expected niche theory keeping smooth well within budget allowed number presences. Combined REML = TRUE select = TRUE, actual complexity penalized , uninformative terms can shrunk zero. Weighted Ensemble: neanderthal data 49 presences. Fitting model predictors might lead overparameterization overfitting. avoid potential issue, fit univariate GAM models presence environmental predictor, ensemble predictions via weighted average, using models’ AUC scores weights.

title: neanderthal
text: code fits ensemble univariate GAM models. following plots show predicted probability Neanderthal presence univariate GAM model. Rug marks along x-axis indicate observed data values.
code: models <- lapply(   X = selection$presence$selection,   FUN = function(x){          mgcv::gam(       formula = as.formula(         paste0(           "presence ~ s(",            x, ",            k = ",            floor(sum(neanderthal$presence)/10),            ")"           )         ),       data = sf::st_drop_geometry(neanderthal),       family = stats::quasibinomial(link = "logit"),       weights = sample_weights,       REML = TRUE,       select = TRUE     )        } )  names(models) <- selection$presence$selection response_plots <- lapply(names(models), function(var) {    #extract individual univariate model   model <- models[[var]]   df <- sf::st_drop_geometry(neanderthal)    #prediction grid   newdata <- data.frame(     x = seq(       from = min(df[[var]]),        to = max(df[[var]]),        length.out = 200)     )   names(newdata) <- var    #predict on link scale with standard errors   pred <- predict(     object = model,     newdata = newdata,      type = "link",      se.fit = TRUE     )    #back-transform to probability scale with 95% CI   newdata$fit <- stats::plogis(pred$fit)   newdata$lower <- stats::plogis(pred$fit - 1.96 * pred$se.fit)   newdata$upper <- stats::plogis(pred$fit + 1.96 * pred$se.fit)    #plot response curve   ggplot(newdata, aes(x = .data[[var]])) +     geom_ribbon(       aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper),       fill = "gray50",       alpha = 0.25     ) +     geom_line(       aes(y = fit),       color = "red4",       linewidth = 0.8     ) +     geom_rug(       data = df[df$presence == 1, ],       aes(x = .data[[var]]),       sides = "t",       color = "red4",       alpha = 0.3,       length = unit(0.1, "npc")     ) +     geom_rug(       data = df[df$presence == 0, ],       aes(x = .data[[var]]),       sides = "b",       color = "gray50",       alpha = 0.3,       length = unit(0.05, "npc")     ) +     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +     labs(x = var, y = "P(presence)") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 11) })  patchwork::wrap_plots(response_plots, ncol = 2)

title: neanderthal
text: section compute partial AUC scores univariate model, global AUC ensemble. honest cross-validation evaluation work better , left simplicity. partial AUC scores used weights building ensemble. scores computed via collinear::score_auc(), function able compute AUC . compute intrinsic AUC model, first generate predictions univariate GAM models, average predictions weighted partial AUC scores, finally compare observations. global AUC score indicates probability presence higher habitat suitability pseudoabsence.
code: model_weights <- lapply(   X = models,   FUN = function(x){          collinear::score_auc(       o = neanderthal[["presence"]],       p = stats::predict(x, type = "response")     )        } ) |>    unlist()  names(model_weights) <- selection$presence$selection #extract predictions from each model predictions <- sapply(   X = models,   FUN = function(x) {     stats::predict(x, type = "response")   } )  #compute weighted average predictions weighted_predictions <- apply(   X = predictions,   MARGIN = 1,   FUN = stats::weighted.mean,   w = model_weights )  #compute ensemble AUC auc <- collinear::score_auc(   o = neanderthal[["presence"]],   p = weighted_predictions )  auc #> [1] 0.923756

title: neanderthal
text: package spatialData provides raster dataset neanderthal_env predict models based neanderthal data. code predicts univariate GAMs neanderthal_env, computes weighted mean produce habitat suitability map.  Finally, combine maps single visualization using whitening approach: suitability color scale progressively bleached toward white uncertainty increases. Vivid colors indicate confident predictions, pale white areas flag regions models disagree.  map suggests Last Interglacial, suitable Neanderthal habitat concentrated southern Europe parts Near East, model confidence decreasing toward range margins data-sparse regions.
code: neanderthal_env <- spatialData::neanderthal_extra() class(neanderthal_env) #> [1] "SpatRaster" #> attr(,"package") #> [1] "terra" names(neanderthal_env) #>  [1] "bio1"                 "bio10"                "bio11"                #>  [4] "bio12"                "bio13"                "bio14"                #>  [7] "bio15"                "bio16"                "bio17"                #> [10] "bio18"                "bio19"                "bio2"                 #> [13] "bio3"                 "bio4"                 "bio5"                 #> [16] "bio6"                 "bio7"                 "bio8"                 #> [19] "bio9"                 "topo_aspect"          "topo_diversity_local" #> [22] "topo_diversity"       "topo_elev"            "topo_slope"           #> [25] "topo_wetness" #predict each model on a separate raster object univariate_predictions <- lapply(   X = names(models),   FUN = function(x) {     terra::predict(       object = neanderthal_env,       model = models[[x]],       type = "response",       na.rm = TRUE     )   } ) |>    terra::rast()  #compute weighted mean of individual predictions ensemble_average <- terra::weighted.mean(   x = univariate_predictions,   w = model_weights )  #weighted standard deviation of the ensemble ensemble_deviation <- terra::app(   x = univariate_predictions,   fun = function(x) {     sqrt(sum(model_weights * (x - weighted.mean(x, model_weights))^2) / sum(model_weights))     }   )  #plot suitability map par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) plot(   x = ensemble_average,   col = viridis::turbo(n = 100),   main = "Weighted Average" ) plot(   x = ensemble_deviation,   main = "Weighted Standard Deviation" ) #combine into a data frame bi_df <- terra::as.data.frame(   x = c(ensemble_average, ensemble_deviation),   xy = TRUE ) names(bi_df)[3:4] <- c("suitability", "uncertainty")  #normalize both variables to [0, 1] bi_df$suit_norm <- (bi_df$suitability - min(bi_df$suitability)) /   (max(bi_df$suitability) - min(bi_df$suitability)) bi_df$unc_norm <- (bi_df$uncertainty - min(bi_df$uncertainty)) /   (max(bi_df$uncertainty) - min(bi_df$uncertainty))  #map suitability to turbo colors turbo_palette <- viridis::turbo(100) base_colors <- col2rgb(   turbo_palette[ceiling(bi_df$suit_norm * 99) + 1] ) / 255  #blend toward white based on uncertainty r <- base_colors["red", ] * (1 - bi_df$unc_norm) + bi_df$unc_norm g <- base_colors["green", ] * (1 - bi_df$unc_norm) + bi_df$unc_norm b <- base_colors["blue", ] * (1 - bi_df$unc_norm) + bi_df$unc_norm bi_df$fill_color <- rgb(r, g, b)  #build map bi_map <- ggplot2::ggplot(   data = bi_df,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, fill = fill_color) ) +   ggplot2::geom_raster() +   ggplot2::scale_fill_identity() +   ggplot2::coord_quickmap() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal() +   ggplot2::labs(x = "", y = "")  #build whitening legend n <- 100 legend_df <- expand.grid(   suitability = seq(0, 1, length.out = n),   uncertainty = seq(0, 1, length.out = n) ) leg_colors <- col2rgb(   turbo_palette[ceiling(legend_df$suitability * 99) + 1] ) / 255 legend_df$fill <- rgb(   leg_colors["red", ] * (1 - legend_df$uncertainty) + legend_df$uncertainty,   leg_colors["green", ] * (1 - legend_df$uncertainty) + legend_df$uncertainty,   leg_colors["blue", ] * (1 - legend_df$uncertainty) + legend_df$uncertainty )  bi_legend <- ggplot2::ggplot(   data = legend_df,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(     x = suitability,     y = uncertainty,     fill = fill   ) ) +   ggplot2::geom_raster() +   ggplot2::scale_fill_identity() +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +   ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Suitability \u2192",     y = "Uncertainty \u2192"   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +   ggplot2::theme(     axis.text = ggplot2::element_blank(),     axis.ticks = ggplot2::element_blank(),     panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank(),     panel.border = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.5)   )  #compose map and legend bi_map + patchwork::inset_element(   p = bi_legend,   left = 0.70,   bottom = -0.1,   right = 1,   top = 0.25 )

title: plantae
text: plantae dataset contains plant diversity metrics 662 global ecoregions, derived 244M+ GBIF plant presence records large set environmental predictors (see plantae_predictors). main features : Complete case study: missing values, clean data ready analysis. Global scope: major biomes biogeographic realms. Multiple responses: 53 response variables (see plantae_responses) covering richness, rarity, beta diversity. Large number predictors: 84 predictors (see plantae_predictors) span 12 categories (temperature, precipitation, atmospheric, soil, fragmentation, human impact, ). dataset designed support wide range ecological modeling approaches.

text: plantae dataset 84 predictors varying levels redundancy. manage multicollinearity plantae use collinear::collinear(). function prioritizes predictors preference_order requested user, remaining ones ranked R-squared univariate random forest models fitted response. predictors evaluated one one selection following ranking: variance inflation factor candidate predictor selected predictors lower 2.5, predictor added selection. code: plantae_selection <- collinear::collinear(   df = plantae,   responses = "richness_species",   predictors = plantae_predictors,   f = collinear::f_count_rf,   preference_order = c(     "bias_log_records", #logarithm of the GBIF records used to compute richness     "geo_area_km2", #ecoregion area     "climate_velocity_lgm_mean", #climate stability since Last Glacial Maximum     "climate_hypervolume", #relative size of the ecoregion's climate envelope     "temperature_mean", #mean temperature     "precipitation_total", #total rainfall     "human_footprint_mean", #average human footprint in the ecoregion     "human_population_density" #inhabitants per square kilometer   ),   max_vif = 2.5,   quiet = TRUE ) plantae_selection$richness_species$selection #>  [1] "bias_log_records"             "geo_area_km2"                 #>  [3] "climate_velocity_lgm_mean"    "climate_hypervolume"          #>  [5] "temperature_mean"             "precipitation_total"          #>  [7] "human_footprint_mean"         "human_population_density"     #>  [9] "fragmentation_dcore_mn"       "human_population"             #> [11] "landcover_herbs_percent_mean" "fragmentation_shape_mn"       #> [13] "geo_neighbors_count"          "fragmentation_ed"             #> [15] "fragmentation_division"       "geo_perimeter_km"             #> [17] "soil_sand"                    "geo_neighbors_area_km2"       #> [19] "fragmentation_contig_mn"      "geo_elevation_mean"           #> [21] "cloud_cover_range"            "fragmentation_cohesion"       #> [23] "precipitation_seasonality"    "geo_distance_to_ocean"        #> attr(,"validated") #> [1] TRUE

title: plantae
text: spatial autocorrelation (SAC) analysis response selected predictors requires distance matrix pairs ecoregions. Something take mind: distance ecoregions may vary depending geometry used computation. distance neighboring ecoregions zero using polygons resulting plantae_extra(), larger zero using point geometry (ecoregion’s centroid) spatialData::plantae. code uses ecoregion centroids spatialData::plantae compute distance matrix kilometers. distance matrix required assess SAC variable plantae. code analyzes SAC response predictors range neighborhood distances.  Notice autocorrelation predictors peaks 1000 km, remains positive across entire distance span. analysis suggests data strong spatial structure, likely suited non-spatial modelling. can transform intuition fact training regular random forest model via spatialRF::rf() assessing spatial autocorrelation residuals. model good performance --bag data (RMSE = 1527.11; R-squared = 0.69), random forest models , let’s take look SAC model residuals.  SAC residuals peaks ~1000km remains positive long distances, indicating model missing predictors accounting spatial structure data.
code: plantae_distance <- sf::st_distance(   x = plantae ) |>    units::set_units(value = "km") |>    units::drop_units() |>    floor()  plantae_distance[1:5, 1:5] #>       [,1]  [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> [1,]     0 14610 15776  3358 12011 #> [2,] 14610     0  9068 14138  9480 #> [3,] 15776  9068     0 16377  5361 #> [4,]  3358 14138 16377     0 15220 #> [5,] 12011  9480  5361 15220     0 plantae_distances_km <- c(100, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000)  spatialRF::plot_training_df_moran(   data = plantae,   dependent.variable.name = "richness_species",   predictor.variable.names = plantae_selection$richness_species$selection,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = plantae_distances_km ) plantae_rf <- spatialRF::rf(   data = sf::st_drop_geometry(plantae),   dependent.variable.name = "richness_species",   predictor.variable.names = plantae_selection$richness_species$selection,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = c(100, 1000, 2000), #more about this later!   verbose = FALSE ) spatialRF::moran_multithreshold(   x = plantae_rf$residuals$values,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = plantae_distances_km,   verbose = FALSE )$plot

text: function spatialRF::rf_spatial() takes model , adds spatial predictors (namely Moran’s Eigenvector Maps MEMs) training data. MEMs synthetic predictors derived distance matrix. MEM captures distinct spatial pattern different scale, broad continental gradients finer regional structures. selecting MEMs reduce residual SAC , spatialRF::rf_spatial() captures spatial variation plant richness environmental predictors alone explain. Think biogeographic legacies, dispersal barriers, unknown environmental gradients. Since used distance.thresholds = c(100, 1000, 2000) non-spatial model plantae_rf, function spatialRF::rf_spatial() generate select spatial predictors neighborhood distances 100, 1000, 2000 km. spatial model performance similar non-spatial one (RMSE = 1512.18; R-squared = 0.7). Now, let’s take look SAC model residuals.  Notice values vertical axis: SAC model residuals still significant distance thresholds 1000 2000km, order magnitude smaller non-spatial model.

title: quercus
text: quercus dataset contains presence absence records eight European Quercus (oak) species combined 31 environmental predictor variables covering bioclimatic, topographic, vegetation, human impact dimensions. main features : Complete case study - missing values, clean data ready analysis. text: predictors indicating climate extreme values identified suffixes _min _max trees_predictors, exception rainfall, rainfall_driest_month rainfall_wettest_month represent monthly minimum maximum respectively. code selects predictors, excludes ndvi_*. candidate pool variables selected, use collinear::collinear() select non-redundant subset prioritizing predictors predictive power response. selected predictors trees : function also returns linear formula ready use, clean dataframe ready analysis.

title: trees
text: conditional inference tree provides interpretable summary selected predictors interact explain tree species richness. model fitted partykit::ctree(), uses permutation tests select splits without overfitting bias. conditional inference tree shows sequence environmental thresholds best explain differences tree species richness. internal node shows splitting variable, threshold, p-value permutation test; terminal node (leaf) shows boxplot richness values samples contains. Interpretation examples results conditional inference trees easy interpret model reasonable number terminal nodes. discuss climate conditions leading highest lowest predicted richness according model. Node 15, right, highest predicted median richness. relevant climate thresholds leading maximum tree richness : Minimum air humidity higher 53%. Rainfall wettest month higher 115mm. Rainfall wettest month higher 451mm. Node 7, third left, shows lowest predicted tree richness, features climate thresholds: Minimum air humidity smaller 53%. Rainfall wettest month higher 104mm. Maximum cloud cover higher 36%.
code: trees_model <- partykit::ctree(   formula = trees_selection$log_trees$formulas$linear,   data = trees |>      sf::st_drop_geometry() |>      na.omit(),   control = partykit::ctree_control(     minbucket = 100 #minimum cases in a terminal leaf   ) ) plot(x = trees_model)

what: Plant diversity metrics for global ecoregions — plantae
title: Plant diversity metrics for global ecoregions — plantae
text: Plant diversity metrics (richness, rarity, beta diversity) obtained GBIF Plantae records global ecoregions. Includes metrics plants, trees, grasses species, genus, family taxonomic levels. Ecoregion boundaries derived Ecoregions 2017. Original polygon geometries converted point centroids reduce file size preserving spatial context. Use plantae_extra() download version original polygon geometries. GBIF download comprised 244,830,168 records 4,741 datasets, filtered records coordinates, geospatial issues, occurrence status "present". Tree species identified cross-referencing GBIF records BGCI Global Tree Search database (BGCI 2020). Grasses defined members family Poaceae. Rarity-weighted richness (Williams et al. 1996): taxon scored inverse number spatial presence records GBIF, scores summed per ecoregion. Mean rarity mean inverse presence record counts per taxon ecoregion. Beta diversity computed ecoregion immediate neighboring ecoregions. Sorensen dissimilarity: Bsor = 1 - 2a/(2a+b+c). Simpson dissimilarity: Bsim = min(b,c)/(min(b,c)+), following Koleff et al. (2003). Fragmentation metrics computed R package landscapemetrics (Hesselbarth et al. 2019) 5 km resolution Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area projection. Climate hypervolume computed using hypervolume::hypervolume_svm(). Aridity computed 1 minus aridity index Trabucco Zomer (2019), maximum aridity coded 1. Environmental predictors extracted mean pixel values per ecoregion rasters 1 km resolution.

what: Global NDVI Records and Environmental Predictors — vi
title: Global NDVI Records and Environmental Predictors — vi
text: sf data frame POINT geometry representing 9,265 global locations 5 response variables encoding Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) 58 environmental predictors (47 numeric, 11 categorical). Use vi_extra() download extended version 30,000 rows. NDVI values derived Copernicus Global Land Service Long Term Statistics product (1999-2019) 1 km resolution. Locations spatially thinned reduce spatial autocorrelation. Response variable encodings: vi_numeric: continuous NDVI values range 0-1. vi_counts: simulated integer counts created multiplying vi_numeric 1000 coercing integer. vi_binomial: simulated binary variable created thresholding vi_numeric 0.5. vi_categorical: character variable categories "very_low", "low", "medium", "high", "very_high", thresholds quantiles vi_numeric. vi_factor: vi_categorical converted factor. Environmental predictors extracted pixel values rasters 1 km resolution.
